Do You Need a Notary to Transfer Property in NC?

Do North Carolina Property Transfers Require Notarization?

If you're buying, selling, or transferring property in the Charlotte area, you've likely wondered whether your deed needs to be notarized. The answer depends on the type of deed, the transaction, and North Carolina law. Understanding these requirements can save you time, money, and legal headaches.

The short answer: most deeds in North Carolina must be notarized to be recorded. However, there are exceptions, and the specifics matter.

What Does North Carolina Law Require?

North Carolina General Statute § 47G-3 states that a deed must be executed in the presence of a notary public (or two witnesses, in limited cases) to be eligible for recording in the Register of Deeds office. Without notarization or proper witnessing, your deed cannot be officially recorded—which means the transfer may not be legally recognized.

This applies to:

  • Warranty deeds (the seller guarantees clear title)
  • Quitclaim deeds (the seller transfers any interest they have, without warranty)
  • Deed in lieu of foreclosure (transferring property to avoid foreclosure)
  • Transfers between family members (gift deeds, inheritance transfers)

Why Notarization Matters for Property Transfers

A notary's role in a property transfer goes beyond simply watching you sign. A certified notary public:

  • Verifies the identity of all parties signing the deed
  • Confirms that signers are signing of their own free will (not under duress)
  • Witnesses the actual signature on the document
  • Applies an official notarial seal and signature, which serves as proof of execution

This verification process protects both buyer and seller. Without it, someone could later challenge the validity of the deed or claim fraud. The Register of Deeds will not accept unnotarized deeds for recording, leaving the property title in limbo.

What a Notary Does NOT Do

It's critical to understand that a notary is not a lawyer and does not provide legal advice, draft deeds, or interpret contracts. If you need help with the language of your deed, title issues, or the terms of a property transfer, consult a real estate attorney. A notary's job is to verify your identity and witness your signature—nothing more.

When You May Not Need Notarization

There are rare exceptions to North Carolina's notarization requirement:

  • Court-ordered transfers: Deeds resulting from court proceedings may not require notarization if executed by a court officer.
  • Two-witness alternative: In rare cases, two disinterested witnesses can substitute for a notary—but this is uncommon and still requires recording rules to be followed.

Even if your deed falls into an exception, recording the deed with the Register of Deeds is essential to protect your property rights.

How to Get Your Deed Notarized in the Charlotte Area

If you're transferring property in Mint Hill, Charlotte, Monroe, or Union County, you can schedule a mobile notary appointment with Integrity Closings CLT. Frank Coxx, a Certified Notary Signing Agent, will come to your home, office, or other location to notarize your deed—no trip to a bank or courthouse required.

To prepare, have on hand:

  • A completed, unsigned deed (prepared by your attorney or title company)
  • A valid photo ID (driver's license, passport, etc.)
  • Any additional documents required by your county's Register of Deeds

Call 980-372-4103 to schedule your appointment or book online.

Final Thoughts

Notarizing your deed is a simple but essential step in any North Carolina property transfer. It ensures your transfer is legal, recordable, and protected. Whether you're buying your first home, gifting property to family, or handling an estate transfer, don't skip this requirement. Work with a trusted mobile notary who understands North Carolina property law and can answer your questions—even if they can't provide legal advice.

For more information on notary services in the Charlotte area, visit our service locations page or learn why families and professionals choose Integrity Closings CLT.
